message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
This is a dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (do NOT flag as bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only, nothing leaves the box), no metered API (subscription OAuth only), engine never imports adapters/datasets (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends are lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; prefer findings you can back with a concrete repro/PoC against the venv. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects (not style/nits). Each finding needs a real file path and, where possible, a line.

You are independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A prior adversarial assessor reported this production-readiness finding:

TITLE: mypy is pinned to `python_version = "3.11"` but CI advertises and matrix-tests up to 3.14, so 3.12–3.14-only type errors are never caught
DIMENSION: ops, packaging & supply chain
CLAIMED SEVERITY: LOW
FILE: pyproject.toml 87
DESCRIPTION: `[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` forces mypy to type-check the code as if running on Python 3.11 regardless of the runner's interpreter. The core CI lane runs mypy across a 3.11–3.14 matrix and the integration lane runs it on 3.12, but because the analysis target is fixed at 3.11, type behavior that differs across versions (e.g. stdlib stubs that gained/changed signatures in 3.12+, version-gated typing constructs) is only ever validated at the 3.11 floor. The project classifies and matrix-tests 3.14 as supported, so the type checker should also exercise the newer targets to make that support real rather than asserted.
SUGGESTED FIX: Either remove the fixed `python_version` so mypy infers the target from the running interpreter (letting the matrix exercise each version), or run an explicit mypy invocation per matrix Python (`mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}`). Keep the lazy-backend overrides as-is.

Independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the actual code; attempt a concrete PoC against the venv if applicable. Set is_real=false if it is a false positive, already-handled, or merely a documented/justified design choice given the hard constraints. Recalibrate severity. Provide your evidence (what you read/ran).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras)
22	        run: |
23	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
24	          pip install -e ".[dev]"
25	      - name: Lint (ruff)
26	        run: ruff check src tests
27	      - name: Format check (ruff)
28	        run: ruff format --check src tests
29	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
30	        run: mypy
31	      - name: Test (pytest)
32	        run: pytest -q
33	
34	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
35	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
36	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
37	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
38	  # e2e) run for real.
39	  integration:
40	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
41	    services:
42	      postgres:
43	        image: postgres:16
44	        env:
45	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
46	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
47	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
48	        options: >-
49	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
50	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
51	      qdrant:
52	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
53	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
54	    env:
55	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
56	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/assay
57	    steps:
58	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
59	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
60	        with:
61	          python-version: "3.12"
62	      - name: Install (all extras)
63	        run: |
64	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
65	          pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"
66	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
67	        run: mypy
68	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
69	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
70	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
71	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
72	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
73	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
74

content.text: The finding is accurate in its facts. Let me verify the claim by testing actual mypy behavior with version-gated typing constructs, to assess whether this is a genuine production-readiness defect.

input.command: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python --version; echo "---mypy---"; /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m mypy --version 2>/dev/null || echo "no mypy in venv"
input.description: Check venv python and mypy versions

content.text: The venv runs Python 3.14, and mypy 2.1.0 is available. Let me construct a concrete PoC: a version-gated type construct that mypy at `python_version=3.11` would treat differently than at 3.12+.

input.command:
cd /tmp && cat > poc_ver.py << 'EOF'
# A construct whose validity depends on the analysis target version.
# PEP 695 type parameter syntax (`class C[T]`) is only valid syntax in 3.12+.
class Box[T]:
    def __init__(self, x: T) -> None:
        self.x = x

def use(b: Box[int]) -> int:
    return b.x
EOF
echo "=== mypy --python-version 3.11 ==="
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m mypy --python-version 3.11 --strict poc_ver.py 2>&1
echo "=== mypy --python-version 3.12 ==="
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m mypy --python-version 3.12 --strict poc_ver.py 2>&1
echo "=== mypy --python-version 3.14 ==="
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m mypy --python-version 3.14 --strict poc_ver.py 2>&1
input.description: PoC: PEP 695 syntax under different mypy target versions

content.content:
=== mypy --python-version 3.11 ===
poc_ver.py:3: error: Type parameter lists are only supported in Python 3.12 and greater  [syntax]
Found 1 error in 1 file (errors prevented further checking)
=== mypy --python-version 3.12 ===
Success: no issues found in 1 source file
=== mypy --python-version 3.14 ===
Success: no issues found in 1 source file

content.text: This confirms the *mechanism* — mypy's `python_version` does change analysis. But the direction matters for production-readiness. Let me check what the codebase actually uses, and critically, what error direction the pinned-floor catches vs. misses.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== PEP 695 type-param syntax usage ===" && grep -rEn 'class +[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*\[' src/ 2>/dev/null | head; grep -rEn '^type +[A-Za-z_]' src/ 2>/dev/null | head; echo "=== match statements / other 3.10+/3.12 features ===" ; grep -rEn 'sys.version_info' src/ 2>/dev/null | head; echo "=== count py files in src ==="; find src -name '*.py' | wc -l
input.description: Check for version-gated constructs in source

      40

content.text:
The code currently uses no PEP 695 type-param syntax, no `type` aliases, no version_info gates. Let me think about the error direction carefully and test the more realistic stdlib-stub scenario the finding cites.

The key production-readiness question: pinning to the 3.11 floor catches errors for the *oldest* supported version. The risk the finding describes is a 3.12+-only type error slipping through. Let me test whether mypy at floor=3.11 misses errors that newer versions would catch, vs. the inverse.

input.command:
cd /tmp && cat > poc_stub.py << 'EOF'
import asyncio

# asyncio.get_event_loop deprecation, and signatures that shifted across versions.
# Test a stdlib symbol removed/changed across versions: typing.ByteString removed in 3.14.
from typing import ByteString  # deprecated 3.9, removed at runtime in 3.14

def f(b: ByteString) -> int:
    return len(b)
EOF
for v in 3.11 3.12 3.13 3.14; do
  echo "=== --python-version $v ==="
  /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m mypy --python-version $v --strict poc_stub.py 2>&1
done
input.description: PoC: typing.ByteString availability across target versions

content.content:
=== --python-version 3.11 ===
Success: no issues found in 1 source file
=== --python-version 3.12 ===
Success: no issues found in 1 source file
=== --python-version 3.13 ===
Success: no issues found in 1 source file
=== --python-version 3.14 ===
Success: no issues found in 1 source file

content.text: That particular symbol isn't flagged by mypy's bundled stubs (mypy uses typeshed, not the live runtime). Let me find a symbol that typeshed actually version-gates, to demonstrate a real false-negative at the 3.11 floor.

input.command:
cd /tmp && cat > poc_new.py << 'EOF'
# itertools.batched was added in Python 3.12. Typeshed gates it with sys.version_info.
from itertools import batched

def f() -> list[tuple[int, ...]]:
    return list(batched([1, 2, 3, 4], 2))
EOF
for v in 3.11 3.12 3.14; do
  echo "=== --python-version $v ==="
  /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m mypy --python-version $v --strict poc_new.py 2>&1
done
echo
echo "##### inverse: a symbol REMOVED in newer versions #####"
cat > poc_removed.py << 'EOF'
# typing.io was removed; test asyncio.coroutine removed in 3.11 (gone). Use a 3.12-removed one:
# 'imp' module removed in 3.12.
import imp  # removed in Python 3.12
EOF
for v in 3.11 3.12 3.14; do
  echo "=== --python-version $v ==="
  /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m mypy --python-version $v --strict poc_removed.py 2>&1
done
input.description: PoC: version-gated stdlib symbols both directions

content.text:
This is the definitive proof of the mechanism in both directions:

- `poc_removed.py` (`import imp`): mypy at floor=3.11 says **"Success: no issues"**, but at 3.12/3.14 it correctly flags `Cannot find ... module named "imp"`. So a real type error that occurs on the supported 3.12-3.14 targets is **silently missed** when `python_version` is pinned to 3.11.

This is exactly the finding's claim: a 3.12+-only type error is never caught because analysis is fixed at the 3.11 floor. The `imp`-removed-in-3.12 case is a concrete, realistic class of defect (deprecated/removed stdlib usage) that the CI matrix advertises as supported but mypy never validates.

Now let me confirm the actual code doesn't already trip this, and check whether there's any other mypy invocation that varies the version.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== any mypy --python-version override anywhere ==="; grep -rn 'python-version\|python_version' .github/ Makefile* tox.ini noxfile.py 2>/dev/null; echo "=== imports of removed/deprecated stdlib in src ==="; grep -rEn '^\s*(import|from)\s+(imp|imghdr|cgi|cgitb|telnetlib|nntplib|aifc|audioop|distutils|asynchat|asyncore)\b' src/ 2>/dev/null || echo "(none found - code is clean today)"
input.description: Check for version-override and risky stdlib imports

content.text:
I have enough to make a calibrated judgment.

**Assessment:**

The finding is factually correct and I reproduced the mechanism with concrete PoCs:
- `python_version = "3.11"` is pinned at pyproject.toml:87; `strict = true`.
- CI `core` lane matrix-tests/type-checks 3.11-3.14 (ci.yml:15, 30); `integration` lane runs mypy on 3.12 (ci.yml:61, 67). Both just call bare `mypy`, which reads the fixed `python_version`, so every runner analyzes as 3.11 regardless of the interpreter.
- pyproject classifiers + `requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"` advertise 3.12/3.13/3.14 as supported.
- PoC: `import imp` (removed in 3.12) → mypy at `--python-version 3.11` reports **"Success: no issues"**, but at 3.12/3.14 correctly errors. So a genuine type/import error real on the advertised newer targets is silently missed. The inverse (`itertools.batched`, added 3.12) and PEP 695 syntax PoCs further confirm the analysis target genuinely shifts behavior.

This is a real gap: type-support for 3.12-3.14 is asserted by the matrix but not actually validated by the type checker.

**Severity recalibration:** LOW is correct, and I'd even consider it on the low end. Today the codebase uses none of the version-gated constructs that would trip this (no PEP 695 syntax, no removed-stdlib imports, no `sys.version_info` gates), and the engine core is dependency-free — so there is no *current* escaped defect, only an under-tightened guard that could let a future 3.12+-only regression through CI. It is a genuine ops/CI correctness gap (the matrix gives false confidence), not cosmetic, but its present impact is latent. LOW.

input.reasoning:
The finding is factually accurate and I reproduced the mechanism. pyproject.toml:87 pins `[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` with `strict = true`. The CI core lane (.github/workflows/ci.yml:15,30) runs a 3.11-3.14 matrix and invokes bare `mypy`; the integration lane (ci.yml:61,67) runs bare `mypy` on 3.12. Because mypy's analysis target is the configured `python_version`, every runner type-checks as if on 3.11 regardless of the interpreter installed. Meanwhile pyproject classifiers and `requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"` advertise 3.12/3.13/3.14 as supported. Net: type behavior that differs on the newer supported targets is never validated — the matrix gives false confidence that 3.12-3.14 type-checking is exercised.

This is a genuine (if low-impact) ops/CI correctness gap, not a style nit and not justified by any hard constraint (the local-first/no-metered-API/lazy-backend constraints are unrelated to the mypy analysis target). It is not already-handled: there is no per-version `mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}` invocation anywhere (grep found none).

Severity is LOW (correctly calibrated, arguably the low end of LOW) because the impact today is purely latent: the source uses none of the constructs that would currently trip a version-divergent error (no PEP 695 type-param syntax, no `type` aliases, no `sys.version_info` gates, no removed/deprecated stdlib imports), and the engine core is dependency-free. So there is no escaped defect right now — only an under-tightened guard that could allow a future 3.12+-only type regression to pass CI undetected.

content.text:
I have completed my independent verification.

**Verdict: TRUE finding, severity LOW (correctly calibrated).**

Key evidence from reading the actual files and running PoCs against the venv (Python 3.14.5, mypy 2.1.0):

- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml:87` pins `[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` with `strict = true`.
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml`: the `core` lane (lines 15, 30) matrix-tests 3.11-3.14 and runs bare `mypy`; the `integration` lane (lines 61, 67) runs bare `mypy` on 3.12. No `--python-version` override exists anywhere (grep confirmed), so every runner analyzes as 3.11.
- pyproject classifiers + `requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"` advertise 3.12/3.13/3.14 as supported.

Decisive PoC (mechanism confirmed in both directions):
- `import imp` (removed in 3.12): `mypy --python-version 3.11` → "Success: no issues"; `--python-version 3.12`/`3.14` → `Cannot find ... module named "imp"`. A genuine error real on the advertised newer targets is silently missed at the pinned floor.
- `itertools.batched` (added 3.12) and PEP 695 `class Box[T]` syntax both confirm the analysis target genuinely changes mypy's verdict.

Why LOW rather than higher: the impact is currently latent. The source uses none of the version-gated constructs that would trip this today (no PEP 695 syntax, no `type` aliases, no `sys.version_info` gates, no removed-stdlib imports), and the engine core is dependency-free. So there is no escaped defect right now — only an under-tightened CI guard that gives false confidence and could let a future 3.12+-only regression pass. No hard constraint justifies the pin.
